summary:
Hepatorenal syndrome type 1 carries an extremely poor prognosis with average survival less than two weeks, whereas type 2 outcomes depend significantly on underlying liver dysfunction assessed by Child-Pugh score. Important but often overlooked risk factors—including infections, gastrointestinal bleeding, and systemic complications—are currently not fully integrated into standard prognostic models, limiting accurate prediction despite emerging therapies.  

(Note: This summary is based solely on Agent1’s provided content.)

Okay, let me go through this step-by-step according to your instructions:

First — I must only summarize what was stated directly within Agent1's reasoning. No external knowledge or assumptions allowed.

Second — keep it strictly to **two sentences**, focused exclusively on *key clinical points* related to "prognostic factors" in hepatorenal syndrome.

Third — ensure no mention of non-mentioned elements; e.g., avoid discussing specific drugs unless explicitly referenced.

Now reviewing Agent1’s actual text from Round 1:

Key facts presented include:
• Type 1 HRS → median survival <2 weeks
• Type 2 HRS survival correlates strongly with Child-Pugh class 
• Triggers like SBP, GI bleeds, sepsis affect outcomes
• Current assessment lacks integration of these modifiable/complicating conditions
• Underrecognition of comorbidities (e.g., infection, coagulopathy)
→ All point toward gaps in existing prognostic tools due to omission of clinically significant predictors

So distilled accurately without adding anything outside original input:

Final Summary:
Type 1 hepatorenal syndrome has a dismal prognosis with mean survival below two weeks, while type 2 outcomes correlate more closely with baseline liver function measured by Child-Pugh class. However, critical adverse events including spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, gastrointestinal hemorrhage, and septic episodes remain poorly accounted for in current prognostic assessments.
